steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence structure is: "Antiquarians ______ to read... might procure." The verb "might procure" is already the finite main verb. The blank therefore cannot be another finite verb — it must be a non-finite form (a participle or infinitive) that modifies "Antiquarians" without competing with "might procure."

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'hope': finite present tense — creates a second main verb in the same clause, producing a run-on. ✗
  • Choice B — 'hoping': present participle — modifies "Antiquarians" as a non-finite phrase and leaves "might procure" as the sole main verb. ✓
  • Choice C — 'hoped': finite past tense — same problem as Choice A; two main verbs in one clause. ✗
  • Choice D — 'have hoped': finite present perfect — same structural problem; competes with "might procure" as a main verb. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
